title: Cloud Computing
tldr: Cloud computing delivers computing services (servers, storage, databases, networking, software) over the internet (the cloud).
Three service models: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service), PaaS (Platform as a Service), SaaS (Software as a Service).
Three deployment models: public cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP), private cloud (on-premises), hybrid cloud (combination).
Key benefits: pay-as-you-go pricing, elastic scaling, global reach, reduced capital expenditure, managed services.
laymanDefinition: Cloud computing is like using a utility company instead of generating your own electricity. Instead of building a power plant (buying servers), you pay for electricity as you use it (pay-as-you-go). You can use more or less without installing new generators (elastic scaling). The utility company handles maintenance (managed services).

deepDive.heading: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)
deepDive.text: Provides virtualized computing resources over the internet. Examples: AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine, Azure VMs. You manage OS, runtime, and applications. Provider manages hardware, virtualization, and networking. Maximum control and flexibility. Best for lift-and-shift migrations.

deepDive.heading: PaaS (Platform as a Service)
deepDive.text: Provides platform to develop, run, and manage applications without infrastructure complexity. Examples: AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Heroku. Provider manages OS, runtime, scaling. You focus on code. Faster development, less operational overhead. Best for web apps and APIs.

deepDive.heading: SaaS (Software as a Service)
deepDive.text: Ready-to-use software delivered over the internet. Examples: Gmail, Salesforce, Slack, Microsoft 365. Provider manages everything. Users just use the application. No installation, maintenance, or upgrades. Pay per user per month. Best for end-user productivity tools.

deepDive.heading: Public vs Private vs Hybrid Cloud
deepDive.text: Public: shared infrastructure, multi-tenant, lowest cost. Private: dedicated infrastructure, single tenant, highest control. Hybrid: mix of public and private, workloads can move between them. Community: shared by several organizations with common concerns. Multi-cloud: using multiple public cloud providers.

deepDive.heading: Cloud Computing Benefits
deepDive.text: Agility: provision resources in minutes. Elasticity: scale up/down automatically. Cost: pay only for what you use (opex vs capex). Reliability: multiple data centers, global reach. Security: shared responsibility model. Managed services reduce operational burden.

interviewAnswer: Cloud computing is the foundation of modern DevOps. Choose the right service model (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS) based on control needs. Leverage elasticity for cost optimization. Understand the shared responsibility model: cloud provider secures the cloud infrastructure, you secure your data and applications.

interviewQuestions.question: What is cloud computing?
interviewQuestions.answer: Delivering computing services over the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing and elastic scaling.

interviewQuestions.question: What are the three cloud service models?
What is IaaS?
interviewQuestions.answer: Virtualized computing resources — you manage OS and apps, provider manages hardware.

interviewQuestions.question: What is PaaS?
interviewQuestions.answer: Platform for developing and running apps without managing infrastructure.

interviewQuestions.question: What is SaaS?
interviewQuestions.answer: Ready-to-use software delivered over the internet. Provider manages everything.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between public and private cloud?
interviewQuestions.answer: Public: shared multi-tenant infrastructure. Private: dedicated single-tenant infrastructure.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the shared responsibility model?
interviewQuestions.answer: Provider secures the cloud infrastructure. Customer secures data, applications, and access within the cloud.

interviewQuestions.question: What is elasticity in cloud computing?
interviewQuestions.answer: The ability to automatically scale resources up or down based on demand.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between capex and opex?
interviewQuestions.answer: Capex: upfront capital expenditure (buying servers). Opex: ongoing operational expenditure (pay-as-you-go cloud).

interviewQuestions.question: What is multi-cloud?
interviewQuestions.answer: Using multiple public cloud providers (e.g., AWS + Azure) for redundancy, best-of-breed, or avoiding vendor lock-in.

codeExamples.title: Provisioning EC2 Instance
codeExamples.code:
# Launch an EC2 instance via CLI
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --instance-type t2.micro \
  --key-name my-key-pair \
  --security-group-ids sg-12345678 \
  --subnet-id subnet-12345678 \
  --tag-specifications
    "ResourceType=instance,
     Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=WebServer}]"
codeExamples.description: Launching an EC2 instance using AWS CLI with basic configuration.

codeExamples.title: Deploying to PaaS (Heroku)
codeExamples.code:
# Deploy a Node.js app to Heroku

# Login
heroku login

# Create app
heroku create my-node-app

# Set environment variables
heroku config:set NODE_ENV=production
heroku config:set DATABASE_URL=postgres://...

# Deploy
git push heroku main

# Scale
heroku ps:scale web=2

# View logs
heroku logs --tail
codeExamples.description: Deploying a Node.js application to Heroku PaaS without managing servers.

codeExamples.title: Cloud Cost Estimation
codeExamples.code:
# AWS Pricing Calculator concepts

Cost factors for EC2:
  - Instance type (t3.medium = $0.0416/hr)
  - Operating system (Windows costs more)
  - Storage (EBS: gp3 $0.08/GB-month)
  - Data transfer ($0.09/GB out)
  - Reserved instances (1yr = 40% savings)

Example: t3.medium web server
  Compute: 730 hrs * $0.0416 = $30.37/mo
  Storage: 50GB gp3 = $4.00/mo
  Data out: 100GB = $9.00/mo
  Total: ~$43.37/mo per instance
codeExamples.description: Understanding cloud cost factors and estimating monthly costs for AWS resources.

codeExamples.title: Shared Responsibility Model
codeExamples.code:
# Shared Responsibility Model

AWS RESPONSIBILITY (Security OF the Cloud)
  - Physical data centers
  - Network infrastructure
  - Hypervisor
  - Global infrastructure

CUSTOMER RESPONSIBILITY (Security IN the Cloud)
  IaaS:
    - OS patches and updates
    - Application security
    - Network ACLs and firewalls
    - Data encryption
  PaaS:
    - Application code security
    - Data access policies
    - User authentication
  SaaS:
    - User access management
    - Data classification
    - Compliance requirements
codeExamples.description: Shared responsibility model mapping what cloud provider vs customer secures.

codeExamples.title: Auto Scaling Group Setup
codeExamples.code:
# Create launch template
aws ec2 create-launch-template \
  --launch-template-name web-template \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --instance-type t3.micro

# Create auto scaling group
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
  --auto-scaling-group-name web-asg \
  --launch-template
    "LaunchTemplateName=web-template,Version=$Default" \
  --min-size 2 --max-size 10 \
  --desired-capacity 2 \
  --vpc-zone-identifier subnet-xxx,subnet-yyy
codeExamples.description: AWS Auto Scaling group configuration for elastic capacity management.

mcqQuestions.question: What are the three cloud service models?
mcqQuestions.explanation: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS are the three service models.

mcqQuestions.question: What does PaaS provide?
options: Platform to develop and run apps
mcqQuestions.explanation: PaaS provides a platform for developing and running applications without managing infrastructure.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the shared responsibility model?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Provider secures the cloud, customer secures their data and applications.

mcqQuestions.question: What is elasticity?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Elasticity automatically scales resources up or down based on demand.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the difference between capex and opex?
options: Capex is upfront, opex is ongoing
Capex is cloud, opex is on-premises
mcqQuestions.explanation: Capex = upfront capital expenditure. Opex = ongoing operational expenditure.

mcqQuestions.question: Which cloud model provides the most control?
mcqQuestions.explanation: IaaS provides the most control over OS, runtime, and configuration.
